Ben Dechrai
Totally. Well, from a security perspective, the first computer I had, well, actually I had like a Texas Instruments 80 or something when I was five.
But the first computer that I hadn't started programming on was an Amsterdam at CPC 6128, which really dates me.
I then had a PC 286, 386, something like that, and ran Windows. And by the time I got to Windows 3.1, my mom would go in and delete icons off the desktop and then I'd have to fix it.
So one of the first things that I built that was actually functional was a DOS based multi user management system that would edit all of the INI files so that my mom wouldn't be able to break things.
But I still have full access when I logged in and all those kind of things.
So I've always been interested in security. And to be fair, that was actually to make my life easier.
But like a seed was planted. I went to university, studied software engineering there and then I lived in London, in London for a while, for a few years before moving to Australia.
And I worked in product companies a lot, but I didn't really like. I enjoy variety and a lot of Change.
So when I worked in agency world advertising agency, it meant that I had a different project all the time, which was exciting, but I didn't really feel like I was doing good for the universe by writing more marketing sites.
I liked the product space, so after I realized that it was the change that I liked, I became a.
I started my own consultancy and I did software engineering, but also security audit, business analysis, solutions architecture, all of those kind of things.
Worked with companies to build products and keep security in mind. And then around the same period of time I was also going to conferences, trying to get to as many as possible.
But obviously every time I went to a conference, that's a week off working for myself, which means a week off making money.
So there was a limit to how much I could do that. And then eventually I met a guy, two or three conferences, not in a row, but over a period of time.
He was a developer advocate@auth0 and he said, look, we're hiring at the moment.
If you really like security, if you like talking to developers and teaching and talking on stage and helping communities and all of those kind of things, you should apply for a job.
Long story short, because I didn't do it, eventually he nagged me so much right for the interview process, got my accountant to tell me whether or not it was a fiscally responsible move, and I took the job and I haven't looked back since.
Um, I've been
MD
Michael D'Angelo
Okay.
BD
Ben Dechrai
running conferences for like 25 years, working with the community in my spare time, but now doing it as my day job is just so fulfilling.
So I, I still code every day. I'm working on side projects because you know, software engineers can never stop, but my heart really is in that community engagement.

So you've got a multi user environment, but those users can also be AI agents.
Each of them has their own Persona and skills and you can actually have conversations with them and, and each conversation is then goal oriented.
If there's a moderator in every chat and if it goes off tangent then it says you should start a thread, come up with conclusion, feed the Results back in here.

I use agentsmd I also use sub agents in Claude quite a bit. That's actually one of the things where I'm finding RALPH can be useful is if you define a good sub agent because the default RALPH instructions is like start up 250 sub agents and run this really quickly which is why I choose you.
But it uses a default sub agent that is just another code instance. So I find that having particular agents can be really useful for that.
If I was slightly less nervous about ralph, I would probably even have tried this in a RALPH loop just to see what happens.
Because I imagine it's probably not going to get it right first time. And what I would do in that situation is do some testing.
And I'm still on the fence about whether or not it's a good idea to use LLMs to write code and then fix it manually.
Especially if you go with spec driven design. Whether it's better to fix a spec and get it to go again.
Because otherwise if you go for the principle that spec driven design should allow you to just wipe the code and start from scratch, if you make manual changes that aren't captured in the spec, then you lose those on the next regeneration.
So I would probably be tempted. There you go. It's got some questions for me.
